explicit_text: I am Sawuya Lubega, originally from Kampala, Uganda, where I completed medical school. In 1991, I came to the United States to conduct research on HIV, but later shifted my focus to clinical medicine, completing a residency in family practice and a fellowship in geriatrics at the University of Minnesota. Throughout my journey, I have lived in several states including Virginia, Indiana, and New York, balancing my medical career with raising my children here. I value community deeply and find fulfillment in caring for entire families through family medicine.

Throughout my journey, I have lived in several states including Virginia, Indiana, and New York, balancing my medical career with raising my children here. I value community deeply and find fulfillment in caring for entire families through family medicine.

Kampala remains the foundation of my personal and professional identity.

explicit_text: I arrived in the United States in 1991 with the goal of conducting HIV research. Over time, I transitioned into clinical medicine, completing my residency and fellowship here, and raising my family in this country. I've lived in several states including New York, Virginia, Indiana, and Minnesota, where I found a welcoming community that made me want to stay. The U.S. has been central to both my professional growth and personal life.

explicit_text: When I first came to the United States in 1991, my main interest was to conduct research on HIV. However, I found that the research opportunities were not quite what I expected, which led me to change my career path towards clinical medicine. Although HIV research was my initial focus, it was the experience that guided me to family practice and geriatrics. This shift was pivotal in shaping my medical career here in the U.S.

explicit_text: I completed a fellowship in geriatrics at the University of Minnesota after finishing my residency in family practice. The university was instrumental in advancing my expertise in caring for older adults, complementing my comprehensive approach to family medicine. I also found the people in Minnesota very welcoming, which influenced my decision to return and settle here after working in other states. The University of Minnesota played a key role in my professional development.
